content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission

August 22, 1990


Benjamin L. Ginsberg
Chief Counsel
Republican National Committee
Dwight D. Eisenhowser Republican Center
310 First Street Southeast
Washington, D.C. 20003

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File Nos.
A-90-491 and A-90-501

Dear Mr. Ginsberg:

This is in response to your request for advice as to whether
the Republican National Committee would be considered a "political
party" committee in California for purposes of the contribution
limitations of the California Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹

QUESTION

The Republican National Committee is a national party
committee established and maintained by the Republican party. If
the Republican National Committee establishes a California
committee for the purpose of making contributions to California
state or local candidates, would that committee be considered a
"political party" committee for purposes of the Act?

CONCLUSION

Assuming the Republican National Committee properly
established a California committee for the purpose of making
contributions to California state or local candidates, that
committee is not a "political party" committee for purposes of the
Act's contribution limits.


________________________
¹ Government Code Sections 81000-91015. All statutory references
are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated. Commission
regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations. Commission
regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations Section
18000, et seq. All references to regulations are to Title 2,
Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations.


428 J Street, Suite 800 • P.O. Box 807 • Sacramento CA 95804-0807 • (916)322-5660

Our File Nos 90-491 and 90-501
Page 2


DISCUSSION

Proposition 73, passed by the California voters on June 7,
1988, for the first time established limits on contributions to
all California state and local candidates, action
committees and political parties.

Under Section 85302, political parties are prohibited from
accepting contributions in excess of $2,500 from a person during
each fiscal year.2 Under Section 85303(b) a political party is
prohibited from contributing more than $5,000 to a candidate in
any fiscal year.

Since the Act does not define the term "political party," the
Commission adopted Regulation 18539 (copy enclosed) in December
1989.3 Subdivision (a) of that regulation defines political
party, for the purposes of the Act's contribution limits, as the
following organizations and committees:

(1) The state central committee or the
statewide governing body of any political
party or of any organization which is
qualified for participation in a primary
election pursuant to Article 2 (commencing
with Section 6430) of Chapter 5 of Division 6
of the Elections Code.

(2) A county central committee
established pursuant to the following
provisions of the Elections Code: Sections
8820 through 8945; Sections 9320 through 9444;
Sections 9700 through 9745; Sections 9830
through 9855; and Section 9955.


2 Section 85102(b) defines "person" as "an individual,
proprietorship, firm, partnership, joint venture, syndicate,
business trust, company, corporation, association, committee and
labor organization." Section 85102(a) defines "fiscal year" as
"July 1 through June 30."

3 Regulation 18539 is in the final rulemaking process and will be
submitted to the California Office of Administrative Law within
the next month. Since the Commission has already adopted this
regulation, it is a statement of Commission policy and staff has
advised pursuant to its provisions.

Our File Nos 90-491 and 90-501
Page 3


     As you correctly stated in your letter, Regulation
18539 does not include committees of national political
parties, such as the Republican National Committee,
within the definition of political party for purposes of
the Act's contribution limits. Absent additional action
by the Commission, we are not authorized to add to the
definitions set forth in the regulation. Consequently,
a California committee established by the Republican
National Committee to make contributions to California
state or local candidates would not be considered a
"political party" committee at this time. Should you
wish to provide additional information to us such as the
requirements for the establishment and recognition of
political parties under federal law and the legal and
political relationship (if any) between national and
California political parties, we can further study the
issue of whether the Act contemplates national political
parties within the coverage of Section 85303(b).

     Finally, please note that the Act does not prohibit
the Republican National Committee from establishing a
California committee to make contributions to California
state or local candidates.⁴ Assuming the appropriate
procedures are followed⁵ and depending on the number of
contributors and candidates supported, such a committee
could operate in California as either a political
committee ($2,500 limit on contributions to each
candidate in a fiscal year; see Sections 85102(c) and
85303(a)) or a broad based political committee ($5,000
limit on contributions to each candidate in a fiscal
year; see Sections 85102(d) and 85303(b)). If the
committee qualifies as a broad based political
committee, the only legal difference between its
contribution activities and those of a political party
committee would be that California candidates could not
control the committee (see Section 82016; Regulation
18539(b), Ferguson Advice Letter No. 86-044, copies
enclosed).


_________________________________________________________________

⁴ The Republican National Committee, however, could not transfer
more than $2,500 of its funds per fiscal year to its new committee
for making contributions to candidates. Transfers of funds for
"purposes other than making contributions to candidates" are not
restricted as to amount. (See Section 85303(c); Regulation 18535,
copy enclosed.)

⁵ For a discussion on the procedures by which a non-California
committee may establish itself in California, see the enclosed
Williams Advice Letter, No. A-89-680.

Our File Nos 90-491 and 90-501
Page 4


If you have further questions regarding this
matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5901.

Sincerely,

Scott Hallabrin
Acting General Counsel

SH:gb

Enclosures

If the Republican National Committee establishes a California committee for the purpose of making contributions to California state or local candidates, would that committee be considered a "political party" committee for purposes of the Act?

Assuming the Republican National Committee properly established a California committee for the purpose of making contributions to California state or local candidates, that committee is not a "political party" committee for purposes of the Act's contribution limits.

, et seq. All references to regulations are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations.
sections.analysis:
Proposition 73, passed by the California voters on June 7, 1988, for the first time established limits on contributions to all California state and local candidates, and to political action committees and political parties.

Under Section 85302, political parties are prohibited from accepting contributions in excess of $2,500 from a person during each fiscal year.2 Under Section 85303(b) a political party is prohibited from contributing more than $5,000 to a candidate in any fiscal year.

Since the Act does not define the term "political party," the Commission adopted Regulation 18539 (copy enclosed) in December 1989.3 Subdivision (a) of that regulation defines political party, for the purposes of the Act's contribution limits, as the following organizations and committees:

(1) The state central committee or the statewide governing body of any political party or of any organization which is qualified for participation in a primary election pursuant to Article 2 (commencing with Section 6430) of Chapter 5 of Division 6 of the Elections Code.

(2) A county central committee established pursuant to the following provisions of the Elections Code: Sections 8820 through 8945; Sections 9320 through 9444; Sections 9700 through 9745; Sections 9830 through 9855; and Section 9955.

2 Section 85102(b) defines "person" as "an individual, proprietorship, firm, partnership, joint venture, syndicate, business trust, company, corporation, association, committee and labor organization." Section 85102(a) defines "fiscal year" as "July 1 through June 30."

3 Regulation 18539 is in the final rulemaking process and will be submitted to the California Office of Administrative Law within the next month. Since the Commission has already adopted this regulation, it is a statement of Commission policy and staff has advised pursuant to its provisions.

As you correctly stated in your letter, Regulation 18539 does not include committees of national political parties, such as the Republican National Committee, within the definition of political party for purposes of the Act's contribution limits. Absent additional action by the Commission, we are not authorized to add to the definitions set forth in the regulation. Consequently, a California committee established by the Republican National Committee to make contributions to California state or local candidates would not be considered a "political party" committee at this time. Should you wish to provide additional information to us such as the requirements for the establishment and recognition of political parties under federal law and the legal and political relationship (if any) between national and California political parties, we can further study the issue of whether the Act contemplates national political parties within the coverage of Section 85303(b).

Finally, please note that the Act does not prohibit the Republican National Committee from establishing a California committee to make contributions to California state or local candidates.4 Assuming the appropriate procedures are followed5 and depending on the number of contributors and candidates supported, such a committee could operate in California as either a political committee ($2,500 limit on contributions to each candidate in a fiscal year; see Sections 85102(c) and 85303(a)) or a broad based political committee ($5,000 limit on contributions to each candidate in a fiscal year; see Sections 85102(d) and 85303(b)). If the committee qualifies as a broad based political committee, the only legal difference between its contribution activities and those of a political party committee would be that California candidates could not control the committee (see Section 82016; Regulation 18539(b), Ferguson Advice Letter No. 86-044, copies enclosed).

4 The Republican National Committee, however, could not transfer more than $2,500 of its funds per fiscal year to its new committee for making contributions to candidates. Transfers of funds for "purposes other than making contributions to candidates" are not restricted as to amount. (See Section 85303(c); Regulation 18535, copy enclosed.)

5 For a discussion on the procedures by which a non-California committee may establish itself in California, see the enclosed Williams Advice Letter, No. A-89-680.

QUESTION: The Republican National Committee is a national party committee established and maintained by the Republican Party. If the Republican National Committee establishes a California committee for the purpose of making contributions to California state or local candidates, would that committee be considered a "political party" committee for purposes of the Act?

CONCLUSION: Assuming the Republican National Committee properly established a California committee for the purpose of making contributions to California state or local candidates, that committee is not a "political party" committee for purposes of the Act's contribution limits.

, et seq. All references to regulations are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations.
